nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; reef flats, outer reef slopes, current-swept channels, and occasionally lagoon reefs at depths of 5–135 m

diet.notes: Generally prefers live foods such as feeder fish and small shrimp; some individuals have been reported to accept frozen meaty foods, particularly when conditioned while young. Fatty feeder fish such as guppies and goldfish have been associated with liver problems and are best avoided. Feeding a few times per week tends to be sufficient given the species' slow metabolism.

summary: The Leaf Scorpionfish (Taenianotus triacanthus) is a master of camouflage from the Indo-Pacific, mimicking a drifting leaf to ambush prey. Its dorsal, anal, and pelvic fins carry venomous spines; handle with caution. Generally kept singly in a 100 L or larger reef aquarium.
careNotes:
The Leaf Scorpionfish (Taenianotus triacanthus) is a highly cryptic ambush predator that remains largely motionless among algae, seagrass, or coral rubble, swaying side-to-side to mimic a drifting leaf. It possesses venomous spines on its dorsal, anal, and pelvic fins; handling with bare hands is strongly discouraged, as stings — while rarely life-threatening — have been reported to cause intense pain and in some cases more serious reactions.

This species moults approximately twice per month, shedding its outer skin layer beginning at the head. Maintaining excellent water quality is generally advised during and around moulting periods. The species occurs in colour variants including red, yellow, brown, and blue, all of which are the same species.

Its aggression is almost entirely predatory: any fish or invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth is at risk, including small crustaceans and ornamental shrimp. Tankmates of similar or larger size tend to be ignored.
